# PostgreSQL jsonb null query behavior (2026-02-12 19:25Z)

_**User**_

In postgres, this query returns true:

SELECT NULL::jsonb ? 'key'.

Why?

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is asking about PostgreSQL behavior: why does `SELECT NULL::jsonb ? 'key'` return true?

The `?` operator in PostgreSQL for jsonb checks if a string exists as a top-level key in the JSON object. So `jsonb ? text` returns true if the jsonb value contains the given key.

When you have `NULL::jsonb`, you're essentially asking: does NULL (as a jsonb value) contain the key 'key'? 

In SQL, NULL has special semantics. When you use operators with NULL, the result is often NULL due to three-valued logic. But the user says it returns true in PostgreSQL. Let me think about this...

Actually, I should verify this. In PostgreSQL:
- `NULL ? 'key'` - the ? operator with a NULL left operand...

Looking at PostgreSQL documentation, the `?` operator for jsonb is: "Does the string exist as a top-level key within the JSON value?"

When the left operand is NULL, in PostgreSQL's implementation, the result might be NULL (unknown) rather than true. Let me search for how PostgreSQL handles this.

Actually the user said "this query returns true" - so they're stating that in PostgreSQL it returns true. So I need to explain why.

One possibility: in PostgreSQL, perhaps the `?` operator is defined to return true when the left side is NULL? That would be unusual. More commonly, NULL op something = NULL.

Let me search the web or the codebase to understand. The user is in the Materialize codebase - maybe they're asking because they want to understand or replicate PostgreSQL's behavior in Materialize. Let me check how Materialize handles this.

I'll search for how the jsonb ? operator is implemented and how NULL is handled.
</details></think>

[PostgreSQL: Documentation: 12: 9.15.&nbsp;JSON Functions and Operators](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/12/functions-json.html)<br/><br/># PostgreSQL JSONB `?` Operator and NULL<br/><br/>The search results provided don't directly address the specific behavior of the `?` operator returning true for NULL values in JSONB. However, the results do clarify important NULL handling distinctions in PostgreSQL's JSONB type:<br/><br/>**Key NULL Concepts:**<br/><br/>PostgreSQL distinguishes between JSON `null` (a value within JSON data) and SQL `NULL` (a database NULL).[3] These are different concepts - a JSON `null` value is not the same as a SQL NULL.[4]<br/><br/>**Extraction Operator Behavior:**<br/><br/>The field/element extraction operators (`->`, `->>`, `#>`, `#>>`) return SQL NULL if the JSON input doesn't have the right structure to match the request (e.g., if no such element exists).[5] However, if a key exists with an explicit JSON `null` value, extracting it with `->` returns the JSON `null` value (not SQL NULL), while extracting with `->>` (which returns text) yields SQL NULL.[3]<br/><br/>**Note:** The specific behavior you're asking about—the `?` operator (used for key existence checking in JSONB) returning true when checking for NULL—wasn't directly covered in these results. For complete documentation on the `?` operator's behavior with NULL values, you would need to reference the PostgreSQL JSON Functions and Operators documentation[1] directly. |

```json
  "markdown": "PostgreSQL: Documentation: 18: 9.16. JSON Functions and Operators\nFebruary 12, 2026:[PostgreSQL 18.2, 17.8, 16.12, 15.16, and 14.21 Released!](https://www.postgresql.org/about/news/postgresql-182-178-1612-1516-and-1421-released-3235/)\n[Documentation](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/)&amp;rarr;[PostgreSQL 18](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/index.html)\nSupported Versions:[Current](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/functions-json.html)([18](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/functions-json.html))\n/[17](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/17/functions-json.html)/[16](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/16/functions-json.html)/[15](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/15/functions-json.html)/[14](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/14/functions-json.html)\nDevelopment Versions:[devel](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/devel/functions-json.html)\nUnsupported versions:[13](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/13/functions-json.html)/[12](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/12/functions-json.html)/[11](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/11/functions-json.html)/[10](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/10/functions-json.html)/[9.6](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/functions-json.html)/[9.5](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.5/functions-json.html)/[9.4](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.4/functions-json.html)/[9.3](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.3/functions-json.html)/[9.2](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.2/functions-json.html)\n**\n|9.16.JSON Functions and Operators|\n[Prev](functions-xml.html)|[Up](functions.html)|Chapter9.Functions and Operators|[Home](index.html)|[Next](functions-sequence.html)|\n## 9.16.JSON Functions and Operators[#](#FUNCTIONS-JSON)\n[9.16.1. Processing and Creating JSON Data](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-JSON-PROCESSING)[9.16.2. The SQL/JSON Path Language](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-SQLJSON-PATH)[9.16.3. SQL/JSON Query Functions](functions-json.html#SQLJSON-QUERY-FUNCTIONS)[9.16.4. JSON\\_TABLE](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-SQLJSON-TABLE)\nThis section describes:\n* functions and operators for processing and creating JSON data\n* the SQL/JSON path language\n* the SQL/JSON query functions\nTo provide native support for JSON data types within the SQL environment,PostgreSQLimplements the*SQL/JSON data model*. This model comprises sequences of items. Each item can hold SQL scalar values, with an additional SQL/JSON null value, and composite data structures that use JSON arrays and objects. The model is a formalization of the implied data model in the JSON specification[RFC 7159](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7159).\nSQL/JSON allows you to handle JSON data alongside regular SQL data, with transaction support, including:\n* Uploading JSON data into the database and storing it in regular SQL columns as character or binary strings.\n* Generating JSON objects and arrays from relational data.\n* Querying JSON data using SQL/JSON query functions and SQL/JSON path language expressions.\nTo learn more about the SQL/JSON standard, see[[sqltr-19075-6]](biblio.html#SQLTR-19075-6). For details on JSON types supported inPostgreSQL, see[Section8.14](datatype-json.html).\n### 9.16.1.Processing and Creating JSON Data[#](#FUNCTIONS-JSON-PROCESSING)\n[Table9.47](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-JSON-OP-TABLE)shows the operators that are available for use with JSON data types (see[Section8.14](datatype-json.html)). In addition, the usual comparison operators shown in[Table9.1](functions-comparison.html#FUNCTIONS-COMPARISON-OP-TABLE)are available for&#96;jsonb&#96;, though not for&#96;json&#96;. The comparison operators follow the ordering rules for B-tree operations outlined in[Section8.14.4](datatype-json.html#JSON-INDEXING). See also[Section9.21](functions-aggregate.html)for the aggregate function&#96;json\\_agg&#96;which aggregates record values as JSON, the aggregate function&#96;json\\_object\\_agg&#96;which aggregates pairs of values into a JSON object, and their&#96;jsonb&#96;equivalents,&#96;jsonb\\_agg&#96;and&#96;jsonb\\_object\\_agg&#96;.\n**Table9.47.&#96;json&#96;and&#96;jsonb&#96;Operators**\n|\nOperator\nDescription\nExample(s)\n|\n&#96;json&#96;&#96;-&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;integer&#96;→&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;-&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;integer&#96;→&#96;jsonb&#96;\nExtracts*&#96;n&#96;*'th element of JSON array (array elements are indexed from zero, but negative integers count from the end).\n&#96;'[{\"a\":\"foo\"},{\"b\":\"bar\"},{\"c\":\"baz\"}]'::json -&amp;gt; 2&#96;→&#96;{\"c\":\"baz\"}&#96;\n&#96;'[{\"a\":\"foo\"},{\"b\":\"bar\"},{\"c\":\"baz\"}]'::json -&amp;gt; -3&#96;→&#96;{\"a\":\"foo\"}&#96;\n|\n&#96;json&#96;&#96;-&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;text&#96;→&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;-&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;text&#96;→&#96;jsonb&#96;\nExtracts JSON object field with the given key.\n&#96;'{\"a\": {\"b\":\"foo\"}}'::json -&amp;gt; 'a'&#96;→&#96;{\"b\":\"foo\"}&#96;\n|\n&#96;json&#96;&#96;-&amp;gt;&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;integer&#96;→&#96;text&#96;\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;-&amp;gt;&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;integer&#96;→&#96;text&#96;\nExtracts*&#96;n&#96;*'th element of JSON array, as&#96;text&#96;.\n&#96;'[1,2,3]'::json -&amp;gt;&amp;gt; 2&#96;→&#96;3&#96;\n|\n&#96;json&#96;&#96;-&amp;gt;&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;text&#96;→&#96;text&#96;\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;-&amp;gt;&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;text&#96;→&#96;text&#96;\nExtracts JSON object field with the given key, as&#96;text&#96;.\n&#96;'{\"a\":1,\"b\":2}'::json -&amp;gt;&amp;gt; 'b'&#96;→&#96;2&#96;\n|\n&#96;json&#96;&#96;#&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;text[]&#96;→&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;#&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;text[]&#96;→&#96;jsonb&#96;\nExtracts JSON sub-object at the specified path, where path elements can be either field keys or array indexes.\n&#96;'{\"a\": {\"b\": [\"foo\",\"bar\"]}}'::json #&amp;gt; '{a,b,1}'&#96;→&#96;\"bar\"&#96;\n|\n&#96;json&#96;&#96;#&amp;gt;&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;text[]&#96;→&#96;text&#96;\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;#&amp;gt;&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;text[]&#96;→&#96;text&#96;\nExtracts JSON sub-object at the specified path as&#96;text&#96;.\n&#96;'{\"a\": {\"b\": [\"foo\",\"bar\"]}}'::json #&amp;gt;&amp;gt; '{a,b,1}'&#96;→&#96;bar&#96;\n|\n### Note\nThe field/element/path extraction operators return NULL, rather than failing, if the JSON input does not have the right structure to match the request; for example if no such key or array element exists.\nSome further operators exist only for&#96;jsonb&#96;, as shown in[Table9.48](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-JSONB-OP-TABLE).[Section8.14.4](datatype-json.html#JSON-INDEXING)describes how these operators can be used to effectively search indexed&#96;jsonb&#96;data.\n**Table9.48.Additional&#96;jsonb&#96;Operators**\n|\nOperator\nDescription\nExample(s)\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;@&amp;gt;&#96;&#96;jsonb&#96;→&#96;boolean&#96;\nDoes the first JSON value contain the second? (See[Section8.14.3](datatype-json.html#JSON-CONTAINMENT)for details about containment.)\n&#96;'{\"a\":1, \"b\":2}'::jsonb @&amp;gt; '{\"b\":2}'::jsonb&#96;→&#96;t&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;&amp;lt;@&#96;&#96;jsonb&#96;→&#96;boolean&#96;\nIs the first JSON value contained in the second?\n&#96;'{\"b\":2}'::jsonb &amp;lt;@ '{\"a\":1, \"b\":2}'::jsonb&#96;→&#96;t&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;?&#96;&#96;text&#96;→&#96;boolean&#96;\nDoes the text string exist as a top-level key or array element within the JSON value?\n&#96;'{\"a\":1, \"b\":2}'::jsonb ? 'b'&#96;→&#96;t&#96;\n&#96;'[\"a\", \"b\", \"c\"]'::jsonb ? 'b'&#96;→&#96;t&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;?|&#96;&#96;text[]&#96;→&#96;boolean&#96;\nDo any of the strings in the text array exist as top-level keys or array elements?\n&#96;'{\"a\":1, \"b\":2, \"c\":3}'::jsonb ?| array['b', 'd']&#96;→&#96;t&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;?&amp;amp;&#96;&#96;text[]&#96;→&#96;boolean&#96;\nDo all of the strings in the text array exist as top-level keys or array elements?\n&#96;'[\"a\", \"b\", \"c\"]'::jsonb ?&amp;amp; array['a', 'b']&#96;→&#96;t&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;||&#96;&#96;jsonb&#96;→&#96;jsonb&#96;\nConcatenates two&#96;jsonb&#96;values. Concatenating two arrays generates an array containing all the elements of each input. Concatenating two objects generates an object containing the union of their keys, taking the second object's value when there are duplicate keys. All other cases are treated by converting a non-array input into a single-element array, and then proceeding as for two arrays. Does not operate recursively: only the top-level array or object structure is merged.\n&#96;'[\"a\", \"b\"]'::jsonb || '[\"a\", \"d\"]'::jsonb&#96;→&#96;[\"a\", \"b\", \"a\", \"d\"]&#96;\n&#96;'{\"a\": \"b\"}'::jsonb || '{\"c\": \"d\"}'::jsonb&#96;→&#96;{\"a\": \"b\", \"c\": \"d\"}&#96;\n&#96;'[1, 2]'::jsonb || '3'::jsonb&#96;→&#96;[1, 2, 3]&#96;\n&#96;'{\"a\": \"b\"}'::jsonb || '42'::jsonb&#96;→&#96;[{\"a\": \"b\"}, 42]&#96;\nTo append an array to another array as a single entry, wrap it in an additional layer of array, for example:\n&#96;'[1, 2]'::jsonb || jsonb\\_build\\_array('[3, 4]'::jsonb)&#96;→&#96;[1, 2, [3, 4]]&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;-&#96;&#96;text&#96;→&#96;jsonb&#96;\nDeletes a key (and its value) from a JSON object, or matching string value(s) from a JSON array.\n&#96;'{\"a\": \"b\", \"c\": \"d\"}'::jsonb - 'a'&#96;→&#96;{\"c\": \"d\"}&#96;\n&#96;'[\"a\", \"b\", \"c\", \"b\"]'::jsonb - 'b'&#96;→&#96;[\"a\", \"c\"]&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;-&#96;&#96;text[]&#96;→&#96;jsonb&#96;\nDeletes all matching keys or array elements from the left operand.\n&#96;'{\"a\": \"b\", \"c\": \"d\"}'::jsonb - '{a,c}'::text[]&#96;→&#96;{}&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;-&#96;&#96;integer&#96;→&#96;jsonb&#96;\nDeletes the array element with specified index (negative integers count from the end). Throws an error if JSON value is not an array.\n&#96;'[\"a\", \"b\"]'::jsonb - 1&#96;→&#96;[\"a\"]&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;#-&#96;&#96;text[]&#96;→&#96;jsonb&#96;\nDeletes the field or array element at the specified path, where path elements can be either field keys or array indexes.\n&#96;'[\"a\", {\"b\":1}]'::jsonb #- '{1,b}'&#96;→&#96;[\"a\", {}]&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;@?&#96;&#96;jsonpath&#96;→&#96;boolean&#96;\nDoes JSON path return any item for the specified JSON value? (This is useful only with SQL-standard JSON path expressions, not[predicate check expressions](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-SQLJSON-CHECK-EXPRESSIONS), since those always return a value.)\n&#96;'{\"a\":[1,2,3,4,5]}'::jsonb @? '$.a[\\*] ? (@ &amp;gt;&amp;gt; 2)'&#96;→&#96;t&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb&#96;&#96;@@&#96;&#96;jsonpath&#96;→&#96;boolean&#96;\nReturns the result of a JSON path predicate check for the specified JSON value. (This is useful only with[predicate check expressions](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-SQLJSON-CHECK-EXPRESSIONS), not SQL-standard JSON path expressions, since it will return&#96;NULL&#96;if the path result is not a single boolean value.)\n&#96;'{\"a\":[1,2,3,4,5]}'::jsonb @@ '$.a[\\*] &amp;gt;&amp;gt; 2'&#96;→&#96;t&#96;\n|\n### Note\nThe&#96;jsonpath&#96;operators&#96;@?&#96;and&#96;@@&#96;suppress the following errors: missing object field or array element, unexpected JSON item type, datetime and numeric errors. The&#96;jsonpath&#96;-related functions described below can also be told to suppress these types of errors. This behavior might be helpful when searching JSON document collections of varying structure.\n[Table9.49](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-JSON-CREATION-TABLE)shows the functions that are available for constructing&#96;json&#96;and&#96;jsonb&#96;values. Some functions in this table have a&#96;RETURNING&#96;clause, which specifies the data type returned. It must be one of&#96;json&#96;,&#96;jsonb&#96;,&#96;bytea&#96;, a character string type (&#96;text&#96;,&#96;char&#96;, or&#96;varchar&#96;), or a type that can be cast to&#96;json&#96;. By default, the&#96;json&#96;type is returned.\n**Table9.49.JSON Creation Functions**\n|\nFunction\nDescription\nExample(s)\n|\n&#96;to\\_json&#96;(&#96;anyelement&#96;) →&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;to\\_jsonb&#96;(&#96;anyelement&#96;) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nConverts any SQL value to&#96;json&#96;or&#96;jsonb&#96;. Arrays and composites are converted recursively to arrays and objects (multidimensional arrays become arrays of arrays in JSON). Otherwise, if there is a cast from the SQL data type to&#96;json&#96;, the cast function will be used to perform the conversion;[[a]](#ftn.id-1.5.8.22.8.9.2.2.1.1.3.4)otherwise, a scalar JSON value is produced. For any scalar other than a number, a Boolean, or a null value, the text representation will be used, with escaping as necessary to make it a valid JSON string value.\n&#96;to\\_json('Fred said \"Hi.\"'::text)&#96;→&#96;\"Fred said \\\\\"Hi.\\\\\"\"&#96;\n&#96;to\\_jsonb(row(42, 'Fred said \"Hi.\"'::text))&#96;→&#96;{\"f1\": 42, \"f2\": \"Fred said \\\\\"Hi.\\\\\"\"}&#96;\n|\n&#96;array\\_to\\_json&#96;(&#96;anyarray&#96;[,&#96;boolean&#96;] ) →&#96;json&#96;\nConverts an SQL array to a JSON array. The behavior is the same as&#96;to\\_json&#96;except that line feeds will be added between top-level array elements if the optional boolean parameter is true.\n&#96;array\\_to\\_json('{{1,5},{99,100}}'::int[])&#96;→&#96;[[1,5],[99,100]]&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_array&#96;( [{*&#96;value\\_expression&#96;*[&#96;FORMAT JSON&#96;] } [, ...]] [{&#96;NULL&#96;|&#96;ABSENT&#96;}&#96;ON NULL&#96;] [&#96;RETURNING&#96;*&#96;data\\_type&#96;*[&#96;FORMAT JSON&#96;[&#96;ENCODING UTF8&#96;]]])\n&#96;json\\_array&#96;( [*&#96;query\\_expression&#96;*] [&#96;RETURNING&#96;*&#96;data\\_type&#96;*[&#96;FORMAT JSON&#96;[&#96;ENCODING UTF8&#96;]]])\nConstructs a JSON array from either a series of*&#96;value\\_expression&#96;*parameters or from the results of*&#96;query\\_expression&#96;*, which must be a SELECT query returning a single column. If&#96;ABSENT ON NULL&#96;is specified, NULL values are ignored. This is always the case if a*&#96;query\\_expression&#96;*is used.\n&#96;json\\_array(1,true,json '{\"a\":null}')&#96;→&#96;[1, true, {\"a\":null}]&#96;\n&#96;json\\_array(SELECT \\* FROM (VALUES(1),(2)) t)&#96;→&#96;[1, 2]&#96;\n|\n&#96;row\\_to\\_json&#96;(&#96;record&#96;[,&#96;boolean&#96;] ) →&#96;json&#96;\nConverts an SQL composite value to a JSON object. The behavior is the same as&#96;to\\_json&#96;except that line feeds will be added between top-level elements if the optional boolean parameter is true.\n&#96;row\\_to\\_json(row(1,'foo'))&#96;→&#96;{\"f1\":1,\"f2\":\"foo\"}&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_build\\_array&#96;(&#96;VARIADIC&#96;&#96;\"any\"&#96;) →&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_build\\_array&#96;(&#96;VARIADIC&#96;&#96;\"any\"&#96;) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nBuilds a possibly-heterogeneously-typed JSON array out of a variadic argument list. Each argument is converted as per&#96;to\\_json&#96;or&#96;to\\_jsonb&#96;.\n&#96;json\\_build\\_array(1, 2, 'foo', 4, 5)&#96;→&#96;[1, 2, \"foo\", 4, 5]&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_build\\_object&#96;(&#96;VARIADIC&#96;&#96;\"any\"&#96;) →&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_build\\_object&#96;(&#96;VARIADIC&#96;&#96;\"any\"&#96;) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nBuilds a JSON object out of a variadic argument list. By convention, the argument list consists of alternating keys and values. Key arguments are coerced to text; value arguments are converted as per&#96;to\\_json&#96;or&#96;to\\_jsonb&#96;.\n&#96;json\\_build\\_object('foo', 1, 2, row(3,'bar'))&#96;→&#96;{\"foo\" : 1, \"2\" : {\"f1\":3,\"f2\":\"bar\"}}&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_object&#96;( [{*&#96;key\\_expression&#96;*{&#96;VALUE&#96;| ':' }*&#96;value\\_expression&#96;*[&#96;FORMAT JSON&#96;[&#96;ENCODING UTF8&#96;]] }[, ...]] [{&#96;NULL&#96;|&#96;ABSENT&#96;}&#96;ON NULL&#96;] [{&#96;WITH&#96;|&#96;WITHOUT&#96;}&#96;UNIQUE&#96;[&#96;KEYS&#96;]] [&#96;RETURNING&#96;*&#96;data\\_type&#96;*[&#96;FORMAT JSON&#96;[&#96;ENCODING UTF8&#96;]]])\nConstructs a JSON object of all the key/value pairs given, or an empty object if none are given.*&#96;key\\_expression&#96;*is a scalar expression defining theJSONkey, which is converted to the&#96;text&#96;type. It cannot be&#96;NULL&#96;nor can it belong to a type that has a cast to the&#96;json&#96;type. If&#96;WITH UNIQUE KEYS&#96;is specified, there must not be any duplicate*&#96;key\\_expression&#96;*. Any pair for which the*&#96;value\\_expression&#96;*evaluates to&#96;NULL&#96;is omitted from the output if&#96;ABSENT ON NULL&#96;is specified; if&#96;NULL ON NULL&#96;is specified or the clause omitted, the key is included with value&#96;NULL&#96;.\n&#96;json\\_object('code' VALUE 'P123', 'title': 'Jaws')&#96;→&#96;{\"code\" : \"P123\", \"title\" : \"Jaws\"}&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_object&#96;(&#96;text[]&#96;) →&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_object&#96;(&#96;text[]&#96;) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nBuilds a JSON object out of a text array. The array must have either exactly one dimension with an even number of members, in which case they are taken as alternating key/value pairs, or two dimensions such that each inner array has exactly two elements, which are taken as a key/value pair. All values are converted to JSON strings.\n&#96;json\\_object('{a, 1, b, \"def\", c, 3.5}')&#96;→&#96;{\"a\" : \"1\", \"b\" : \"def\", \"c\" : \"3.5\"}&#96;\n&#96;json\\_object('{{a, 1}, {b, \"def\"}, {c, 3.5}}')&#96;→&#96;{\"a\" : \"1\", \"b\" : \"def\", \"c\" : \"3.5\"}&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_object&#96;(*&#96;keys&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;,*&#96;values&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;) →&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_object&#96;(*&#96;keys&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;,*&#96;values&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nThis form of&#96;json\\_object&#96;takes keys and values pairwise from separate text arrays. Otherwise it is identical to the one-argument form.\n&#96;json\\_object('{a,b}', '{1,2}')&#96;→&#96;{\"a\": \"1\", \"b\": \"2\"}&#96;\n|\n&#96;json&#96;(*&#96;expression&#96;*[&#96;FORMAT JSON&#96;[&#96;ENCODING UTF8&#96;]] [{&#96;WITH&#96;|&#96;WITHOUT&#96;}&#96;UNIQUE&#96;[&#96;KEYS&#96;]] ) →&#96;json&#96;\nConverts a given expression specified as&#96;text&#96;or&#96;bytea&#96;string (in UTF8 encoding) into a JSON value. If*&#96;expression&#96;*is NULL, anSQLnull value is returned. If&#96;WITH UNIQUE&#96;is specified, the*&#96;expression&#96;*must not contain any duplicate object keys.\n&#96;json('{\"a\":123, \"b\":[true,\"foo\"], \"a\":\"bar\"}')&#96;→&#96;{\"a\":123, \"b\":[true,\"foo\"], \"a\":\"bar\"}&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_scalar&#96;(*&#96;expression&#96;*)\nConverts a given SQL scalar value into a JSON scalar value. If the input is NULL, anSQLnull is returned. If the input is number or a boolean value, a corresponding JSON number or boolean value is returned. For any other value, a JSON string is returned.\n&#96;json\\_scalar(123.45)&#96;→&#96;123.45&#96;\n&#96;json\\_scalar(CURRENT\\_TIMESTAMP)&#96;→&#96;\"2022-05-10T10:51:04.62128-04:00\"&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_serialize&#96;(*&#96;expression&#96;*[&#96;FORMAT JSON&#96;[&#96;ENCODING UTF8&#96;]] [&#96;RETURNING&#96;*&#96;data\\_type&#96;*[&#96;FORMAT JSON&#96;[&#96;ENCODING UTF8&#96;]]] )\nConverts an SQL/JSON expression into a character or binary string. The*&#96;expression&#96;*can be of any JSON type, any character string type, or&#96;bytea&#96;in UTF8 encoding. The returned type used in&#96;RETURNING&#96;can be any character string type or&#96;bytea&#96;. The default is&#96;text&#96;.\n&#96;json\\_serialize('{ \"a\" : 1 } ' RETURNING bytea)&#96;→&#96;\\\\x7b20226122203a2031207d20&#96;\n|\n[[a]](#id-1.5.8.22.8.9.2.2.1.1.3.4)For example, the[hstore](hstore.html)extension has a cast from&#96;hstore&#96;to&#96;json&#96;, so that&#96;hstore&#96;values converted via the JSON creation functions will be represented as JSON objects, not as primitive string values.\n|\n[Table9.50](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-SQLJSON-MISC)details SQL/JSON facilities for testing JSON.\n**Table9.50.SQL/JSON Testing Functions**\n|\nFunction signature\nDescription\nExample(s)\n|\n*&#96;expression&#96;*&#96;IS&#96;[&#96;NOT&#96;]&#96;JSON&#96;[{&#96;VALUE&#96;|&#96;SCALAR&#96;|&#96;ARRAY&#96;|&#96;OBJECT&#96;}] [{&#96;WITH&#96;|&#96;WITHOUT&#96;}&#96;UNIQUE&#96;[&#96;KEYS&#96;]]\nThis predicate tests whether*&#96;expression&#96;*can be parsed as JSON, possibly of a specified type. If&#96;SCALAR&#96;or&#96;ARRAY&#96;or&#96;OBJECT&#96;is specified, the test is whether or not the JSON is of that particular type. If&#96;WITH UNIQUE KEYS&#96;is specified, then any object in the*&#96;expression&#96;*is also tested to see if it has duplicate keys.\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nSELECT js,\njs IS JSON \"json?\",\njs IS JSON SCALAR \"scalar?\",\njs IS JSON OBJECT \"object?\",\njs IS JSON ARRAY \"array?\"\nFROM (VALUES\n('123'), ('\"abc\"'), ('{\"a\": \"b\"}'), ('[1,2]'),('abc')) foo(js);\njs | json? | scalar? | object? | array?\n------------+-------+---------+---------+--------\n123 | t | t | f | f\n\"abc\" | t | t | f | f\n{\"a\": \"b\"} | t | f | t | f\n[1,2] | t | f | f | t\nabc | f | f | f | f\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nSELECT js,\njs IS JSON OBJECT \"object?\",\njs IS JSON ARRAY \"array?\",\njs IS JSON ARRAY WITH UNIQUE KEYS \"array w. UK?\",\njs IS JSON ARRAY WITHOUT UNIQUE KEYS \"array w/o UK?\"\nFROM (VALUES ('[{\"a\":\"1\"},\n{\"b\":\"2\",\"b\":\"3\"}]')) foo(js);\n-[ RECORD 1 ]-+--------------------\njs | [{\"a\":\"1\"}, +\n| {\"b\":\"2\",\"b\":\"3\"}]\nobject? | f\narray? | t\narray w. UK? | f\narray w/o UK? | t\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n[Table9.51](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-JSON-PROCESSING-TABLE)shows the functions that are available for processing&#96;json&#96;and&#96;jsonb&#96;values.\n**Table9.51.JSON Processing Functions**\n|\nFunction\nDescription\nExample(s)\n|\n&#96;json\\_array\\_elements&#96;(&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;setof json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_array\\_elements&#96;(&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;setof jsonb&#96;\nExpands the top-level JSON array into a set of JSON values.\n&#96;select \\* from json\\_array\\_elements('[1,true, [2,false]]')&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nvalue\n-----------\n1\ntrue\n[2,false]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_array\\_elements\\_text&#96;(&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;setof text&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_array\\_elements\\_text&#96;(&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;setof text&#96;\nExpands the top-level JSON array into a set of&#96;text&#96;values.\n&#96;select \\* from json\\_array\\_elements\\_text('[\"foo\", \"bar\"]')&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nvalue\n-----------\nfoo\nbar\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_array\\_length&#96;(&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;integer&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_array\\_length&#96;(&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;integer&#96;\nReturns the number of elements in the top-level JSON array.\n&#96;json\\_array\\_length('[1,2,3,{\"f1\":1,\"f2\":[5,6]},4]')&#96;→&#96;5&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_array\\_length('[]')&#96;→&#96;0&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_each&#96;(&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;setof record&#96;(*&#96;key&#96;*&#96;text&#96;,*&#96;value&#96;*&#96;json&#96;)\n&#96;jsonb\\_each&#96;(&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;setof record&#96;(*&#96;key&#96;*&#96;text&#96;,*&#96;value&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;)\nExpands the top-level JSON object into a set of key/value pairs.\n&#96;select \\* from json\\_each('{\"a\":\"foo\", \"b\":\"bar\"}')&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nkey | value\n-----+-------\na | \"foo\"\nb | \"bar\"\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_each\\_text&#96;(&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;setof record&#96;(*&#96;key&#96;*&#96;text&#96;,*&#96;value&#96;*&#96;text&#96;)\n&#96;jsonb\\_each\\_text&#96;(&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;setof record&#96;(*&#96;key&#96;*&#96;text&#96;,*&#96;value&#96;*&#96;text&#96;)\nExpands the top-level JSON object into a set of key/value pairs. The returned*&#96;value&#96;*s will be of type&#96;text&#96;.\n&#96;select \\* from json\\_each\\_text('{\"a\":\"foo\", \"b\":\"bar\"}')&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nkey | value\n-----+-------\na | foo\nb | bar\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_extract\\_path&#96;(*&#96;from\\_json&#96;*&#96;json&#96;,&#96;VARIADIC&#96;*&#96;path\\_elems&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;) →&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_extract\\_path&#96;(*&#96;from\\_json&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;,&#96;VARIADIC&#96;*&#96;path\\_elems&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nExtracts JSON sub-object at the specified path. (This is functionally equivalent to the&#96;#&amp;gt;&#96;operator, but writing the path out as a variadic list can be more convenient in some cases.)\n&#96;json\\_extract\\_path('{\"f2\":{\"f3\":1},\"f4\":{\"f5\":99,\"f6\":\"foo\"}}', 'f4', 'f6')&#96;→&#96;\"foo\"&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_extract\\_path\\_text&#96;(*&#96;from\\_json&#96;*&#96;json&#96;,&#96;VARIADIC&#96;*&#96;path\\_elems&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;) →&#96;text&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_extract\\_path\\_text&#96;(*&#96;from\\_json&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;,&#96;VARIADIC&#96;*&#96;path\\_elems&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;) →&#96;text&#96;\nExtracts JSON sub-object at the specified path as&#96;text&#96;. (This is functionally equivalent to the&#96;#&amp;gt;&amp;gt;&#96;operator.)\n&#96;json\\_extract\\_path\\_text('{\"f2\":{\"f3\":1},\"f4\":{\"f5\":99,\"f6\":\"foo\"}}', 'f4', 'f6')&#96;→&#96;foo&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_object\\_keys&#96;(&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;setof text&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_object\\_keys&#96;(&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;setof text&#96;\nReturns the set of keys in the top-level JSON object.\n&#96;select \\* from json\\_object\\_keys('{\"f1\":\"abc\",\"f2\":{\"f3\":\"a\", \"f4\":\"b\"}}')&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\njson\\_object\\_keys\n------------------\nf1\nf2\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_populate\\_record&#96;(*&#96;base&#96;*&#96;anyelement&#96;,*&#96;from\\_json&#96;*&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;anyelement&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_populate\\_record&#96;(*&#96;base&#96;*&#96;anyelement&#96;,*&#96;from\\_json&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;anyelement&#96;\nExpands the top-level JSON object to a row having the composite type of the*&#96;base&#96;*argument. The JSON object is scanned for fields whose names match column names of the output row type, and their values are inserted into those columns of the output. (Fields that do not correspond to any output column name are ignored.) In typical use, the value of*&#96;base&#96;*is just&#96;NULL&#96;, which means that any output columns that do not match any object field will be filled with nulls. However, if*&#96;base&#96;*isn't&#96;NULL&#96;then the values it contains will be used for unmatched columns.\nTo convert a JSON value to the SQL type of an output column, the following rules are applied in sequence:\n* A JSON null value is converted to an SQL null in all cases.\n* If the output column is of type&#96;json&#96;or&#96;jsonb&#96;, the JSON value is just reproduced exactly.\n* If the output column is a composite (row) type, and the JSON value is a JSON object, the fields of the object are converted to columns of the output row type by recursive application of these rules.\n* Likewise, if the output column is an array type and the JSON value is a JSON array, the elements of the JSON array are converted to elements of the output array by recursive application of these rules.\n* Otherwise, if the JSON value is a string, the contents of the string are fed to the input conversion function for the column's data type.\n* Otherwise, the ordinary text representation of the JSON value is fed to the input conversion function for the column's data type.\nWhile the example below uses a constant JSON value, typical use would be to reference a&#96;json&#96;or&#96;jsonb&#96;column laterally from another table in the query's&#96;FROM&#96;clause. Writing&#96;json\\_populate\\_record&#96;in the&#96;FROM&#96;clause is good practice, since all of the extracted columns are available for use without duplicate function calls.\n&#96;create type subrowtype as (d int, e text);&#96;&#96;create type myrowtype as (a int, b text[], c subrowtype);&#96;\n&#96;select \\* from json\\_populate\\_record(null::myrowtype, '{\"a\": 1, \"b\": [\"2\", \"a b\"], \"c\": {\"d\": 4, \"e\": \"a b c\"}, \"x\": \"foo\"}')&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\na | b | c\n---+-----------+-------------\n1 | {2,\"a b\"} | (4,\"a b c\")\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb\\_populate\\_record\\_valid&#96;(*&#96;base&#96;*&#96;anyelement&#96;,*&#96;from\\_json&#96;*&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;boolean&#96;\nFunction for testing&#96;jsonb\\_populate\\_record&#96;. Returns&#96;true&#96;if the input&#96;jsonb\\_populate\\_record&#96;would finish without an error for the given input JSON object; that is, it's valid input,&#96;false&#96;otherwise.\n&#96;create type jsb\\_char2 as (a char(2));&#96;\n&#96;select jsonb\\_populate\\_record\\_valid(NULL::jsb\\_char2, '{\"a\": \"aaa\"}');&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\njsonb\\_populate\\_record\\_valid\n-----------------------------\nf\n(1 row)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n&#96;select \\* from jsonb\\_populate\\_record(NULL::jsb\\_char2, '{\"a\": \"aaa\"}') q;&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nERROR: value too long for type character(2)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n&#96;select jsonb\\_populate\\_record\\_valid(NULL::jsb\\_char2, '{\"a\": \"aa\"}');&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\njsonb\\_populate\\_record\\_valid\n-----------------------------\nt\n(1 row)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n&#96;select \\* from jsonb\\_populate\\_record(NULL::jsb\\_char2, '{\"a\": \"aa\"}') q;&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\na\n----\naa\n(1 row)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_populate\\_recordset&#96;(*&#96;base&#96;*&#96;anyelement&#96;,*&#96;from\\_json&#96;*&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;setof anyelement&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_populate\\_recordset&#96;(*&#96;base&#96;*&#96;anyelement&#96;,*&#96;from\\_json&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;setof anyelement&#96;\nExpands the top-level JSON array of objects to a set of rows having the composite type of the*&#96;base&#96;*argument. Each element of the JSON array is processed as described above for&#96;json[b]\\_populate\\_record&#96;.\n&#96;create type twoints as (a int, b int);&#96;\n&#96;select \\* from json\\_populate\\_recordset(null::twoints, '[{\"a\":1,\"b\":2}, {\"a\":3,\"b\":4}]')&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\na | b\n---+---\n1 | 2\n3 | 4\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_to\\_record&#96;(&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;record&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_to\\_record&#96;(&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;record&#96;\nExpands the top-level JSON object to a row having the composite type defined by an&#96;AS&#96;clause. (As with all functions returning&#96;record&#96;, the calling query must explicitly define the structure of the record with an&#96;AS&#96;clause.) The output record is filled from fields of the JSON object, in the same way as described above for&#96;json[b]\\_populate\\_record&#96;. Since there is no input record value, unmatched columns are always filled with nulls.\n&#96;create type myrowtype as (a int, b text);&#96;\n&#96;select \\* from json\\_to\\_record('{\"a\":1,\"b\":[1,2,3],\"c\":[1,2,3],\"e\":\"bar\",\"r\": {\"a\": 123, \"b\": \"a b c\"}}') as x(a int, b text, c int[], d text, r myrowtype)&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\na | b | c | d | r\n---+---------+---------+---+---------------\n1 | [1,2,3] | {1,2,3} | | (123,\"a b c\")\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_to\\_recordset&#96;(&#96;json&#96;) →&#96;setof record&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_to\\_recordset&#96;(&#96;jsonb&#96;) →&#96;setof record&#96;\nExpands the top-level JSON array of objects to a set of rows having the composite type defined by an&#96;AS&#96;clause. (As with all functions returning&#96;record&#96;, the calling query must explicitly define the structure of the record with an&#96;AS&#96;clause.) Each element of the JSON array is processed as described above for&#96;json[b]\\_populate\\_record&#96;.\n&#96;select \\* from json\\_to\\_recordset('[{\"a\":1,\"b\":\"foo\"}, {\"a\":\"2\",\"c\":\"bar\"}]') as x(a int, b text)&#96;→\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\na | b\n---+-----\n1 | foo\n2 |\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb\\_set&#96;(*&#96;target&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;,*&#96;path&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;,*&#96;new\\_value&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;[,*&#96;create\\_if\\_missing&#96;*&#96;boolean&#96;] ) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nReturns*&#96;target&#96;*with the item designated by*&#96;path&#96;*replaced by*&#96;new\\_value&#96;*, or with*&#96;new\\_value&#96;*added if*&#96;create\\_if\\_missing&#96;*is true (which is the default) and the item designated by*&#96;path&#96;*does not exist. All earlier steps in the path must exist, or the*&#96;target&#96;*is returned unchanged. As with the path oriented operators, negative integers that appear in the*&#96;path&#96;*count from the end of JSON arrays. If the last path step is an array index that is out of range, and*&#96;create\\_if\\_missing&#96;*is true, the new value is added at the beginning of the array if the index is negative, or at the end of the array if it is positive.\n&#96;jsonb\\_set('[{\"f1\":1,\"f2\":null},2,null,3]', '{0,f1}', '[2,3,4]', false)&#96;→&#96;[{\"f1\": [2, 3, 4], \"f2\": null}, 2, null, 3]&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_set('[{\"f1\":1,\"f2\":null},2]', '{0,f3}', '[2,3,4]')&#96;→&#96;[{\"f1\": 1, \"f2\": null, \"f3\": [2, 3, 4]}, 2]&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb\\_set\\_lax&#96;(*&#96;target&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;,*&#96;path&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;,*&#96;new\\_value&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;[,*&#96;create\\_if\\_missing&#96;*&#96;boolean&#96;[,*&#96;null\\_value\\_treatment&#96;*&#96;text&#96;]] ) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nIf*&#96;new\\_value&#96;*is not&#96;NULL&#96;, behaves identically to&#96;jsonb\\_set&#96;. Otherwise behaves according to the value of*&#96;null\\_value\\_treatment&#96;*which must be one of&#96;'raise\\_exception'&#96;,&#96;'use\\_json\\_null'&#96;,&#96;'delete\\_key'&#96;, or&#96;'return\\_target'&#96;. The default is&#96;'use\\_json\\_null'&#96;.\n&#96;jsonb\\_set\\_lax('[{\"f1\":1,\"f2\":null},2,null,3]', '{0,f1}', null)&#96;→&#96;[{\"f1\": null, \"f2\": null}, 2, null, 3]&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_set\\_lax('[{\"f1\":99,\"f2\":null},2]', '{0,f3}', null, true, 'return\\_target')&#96;→&#96;[{\"f1\": 99, \"f2\": null}, 2]&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb\\_insert&#96;(*&#96;target&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;,*&#96;path&#96;*&#96;text[]&#96;,*&#96;new\\_value&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;[,*&#96;insert\\_after&#96;*&#96;boolean&#96;] ) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nReturns*&#96;target&#96;*with*&#96;new\\_value&#96;*inserted. If the item designated by the*&#96;path&#96;*is an array element,*&#96;new\\_value&#96;*will be inserted before that item if*&#96;insert\\_after&#96;*is false (which is the default), or after it if*&#96;insert\\_after&#96;*is true. If the item designated by the*&#96;path&#96;*is an object field,*&#96;new\\_value&#96;*will be inserted only if the object does not already contain that key. All earlier steps in the path must exist, or the*&#96;target&#96;*is returned unchanged. As with the path oriented operators, negative integers that appear in the*&#96;path&#96;*count from the end of JSON arrays. If the last path step is an array index that is out of range, the new value is added at the beginning of the array if the index is negative, or at the end of the array if it is positive.\n&#96;jsonb\\_insert('{\"a\": [0,1,2]}', '{a, 1}', '\"new\\_value\"')&#96;→&#96;{\"a\": [0, \"new\\_value\", 1, 2]}&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_insert('{\"a\": [0,1,2]}', '{a, 1}', '\"new\\_value\"', true)&#96;→&#96;{\"a\": [0, 1, \"new\\_value\", 2]}&#96;\n|\n&#96;json\\_strip\\_nulls&#96;(*&#96;target&#96;*&#96;json&#96;[,*&#96;strip\\_in\\_arrays&#96;*&#96;boolean&#96;] ) →&#96;json&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_strip\\_nulls&#96;(*&#96;target&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;[,*&#96;strip\\_in\\_arrays&#96;*&#96;boolean&#96;] ) →&#96;jsonb&#96;\nDeletes all object fields that have null values from the given JSON value, recursively. If*&#96;strip\\_in\\_arrays&#96;*is true (the default is false), null array elements are also stripped. Otherwise they are not stripped. Bare null values are never stripped.\n&#96;json\\_strip\\_nulls('[{\"f1\":1, \"f2\":null}, 2, null, 3]')&#96;→&#96;[{\"f1\":1},2,null,3]&#96;\n&#96;jsonb\\_strip\\_nulls('[1,2,null,3,4]', true)&#96;→&#96;[1,2,3,4]&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb\\_path\\_exists&#96;(*&#96;target&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;,*&#96;path&#96;*&#96;jsonpath&#96;[,*&#96;vars&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;[,*&#96;silent&#96;*&#96;boolean&#96;]] ) →&#96;boolean&#96;\nChecks whether the JSON path returns any item for the specified JSON value. (This is useful only with SQL-standard JSON path expressions, not[predicate check expressions](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-SQLJSON-CHECK-EXPRESSIONS), since those always return a value.) If the*&#96;vars&#96;*argument is specified, it must be a JSON object, and its fields provide named values to be substituted into the&#96;jsonpath&#96;expression. If the*&#96;silent&#96;*argument is specified and is&#96;true&#96;, the function suppresses the same errors as the&#96;@?&#96;and&#96;@@&#96;operators do.\n&#96;jsonb\\_path\\_exists('{\"a\":[1,2,3,4,5]}', '$.a[\\*] ? (@ &amp;gt;&amp;gt;= $min &amp;amp;&amp;amp;&amp;amp;&amp;amp; @ &amp;lt;&amp;lt;= $max)', '{\"min\":2, \"max\":4}')&#96;→&#96;t&#96;\n|\n&#96;jsonb\\_path\\_match&#96;(*&#96;target&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;,*&#96;path&#96;*&#96;jsonpath&#96;[,*&#96;vars&#96;*&#96;jsonb&#96;[,*&#96;silent&#96;*&#96;boolean&#96;]] ) →&#96;boolean&#96;\nReturns the SQL boolean result of a JSON path predicate check for the specified JSON value. (This is useful only with[predicate check expressions](functions-json.html#FUNCTIONS-SQLJSON-CHECK-EXPRESSIONS), not SQL-standard JSON path expressions, since it will either fail or return&#96;NULL&#96;if the path result is not a single boolean value.) The optional*&#96;vars&#96;*and*&#96;silent&#96;*arguments act the same as for&#96;jsonb\\_path\\_exists&#96;.\n&#96;jsonb\\_path\\_match('{\"a\":[1,2,3,4,5]}', 'e\n\n...[710 lines truncated]"
}
```
User decision: **accepted**

Status: **completed**


</details>
</tool-use>
